decisions.prompt: The first-week binder is empty. Your sister-in-law swears by the boxed curriculum — open it, follow the script. Or you could write the plan yourself.

options.label: Open the box. Follow the plan.
options.flavor: The plan got you through the week. Some of the read-alouds you wouldn't have chosen. Hazel didn't mind. You did, quietly, while Hazel was laughing at the read-alouds.

options.label: Write your own plan
scene: A schedule that fits your kids.
options.flavor: Eleven hours of planning. The week ran. Three lessons hit, two didn't, and you knew exactly which were which by Friday.

decisions.prompt: A neighborhood co-op meets Tuesday mornings. Five families, shared lessons, a craft and a snack. The kids get friends. You get adult conversation. You cannot, when you really think about it, find a downside.

options.label: Sign on
options.flavor: Bram found a friend. Hazel found two. You found a parent you actually like. Tuesday mornings, gone — and you don't quite miss them.

options.label: Stay solo for now
options.flavor: You said maybe next year. The group chat looked, frankly, like a lot. You'd survived three months on your own. You could survive a few more, probably.

decisions.prompt: The state wants an end-of-year evaluation. You can put Hazel through a standardized test, or pay an evaluator to write a portfolio review instead.

options.label: Sit the test
scene: Three hours and a score sheet.
options.flavor: She scored above grade in two subjects, below in one. The number was somehow both a relief and a verdict. You knew exactly what to work on by Monday. Math. It is, somehow, always math.

options.label: Hire the evaluator
options.flavor: The evaluator was kind. The portfolio narrative used the word 'curious' three times. You weren't sure if that was teaching or marketing.

decisions.prompt: An old coworker has a remote contract that fits — twenty-five hours a week, decent rate. Bram is finally old enough to read in the other room. The work would not get done at the kitchen table.

options.label: Take the contract
scene: A locked door, an hour at a time.
A paycheck with your name on it.
options.flavor: The work was good. You were good at it. Hazel knocked on the door once and you said 'in a minute' and then forgot for forty. You didn't forget that you'd forgotten.

options.label: Pass for now
options.flavor: You wrote a careful no. The coworker said they understood. The version of you they remembered would have said yes in twelve minutes. The current version was reading 'There Was An Old Lady Who Swallowed A Fly' for the ninth time. Different version. Different fly.

decisions.prompt: Hazel is twelve. She asks, calmly, if she can go to school in the fall. She has thought about it. She is not asking permission so much as telling you where she's heading.

options.label: Sign her up
scene: A kitchen with one student in it.
options.flavor: She walked in like she'd been planning it for a year. Maybe she had been. The kitchen at 9am was quiet in a way you weren't ready for. You got into the quiet eventually. The quiet got into you too.

options.label: Make the case for one more year
scene: A compromise neither of you loves.
options.flavor: She stayed. The year was fine. Something in how she looked at you on Tuesdays didn't quite return. You noticed. She noticed you noticing. Tuesdays got, somehow, longer.

decisions.prompt: Hour seven of indoor weather. The screen rule you set on Monday has not survived to Thursday. You can let it go for the week, or you can hold the line.

options.label: Let it go this week
options.flavor: You let them watch the show twice through. You sat next to Bram for the second one. You both knew the dialogue. He recited it to you in the car the next day.

options.label: Hold the line
options.flavor: The first hour was the hard one. By the third, they were drawing on the kitchen floor with sidewalk chalk you didn't remember buying. You let that part go.

decisions.prompt: Your mother-in-law is visiting Friday. She has views about the kids' schooling. She has already said 'just asking, of course' on the phone.

options.label: Set the table and the terms
options.flavor: You named the curriculum. You showed her the math workbook. She nodded. She brought it up again at dessert. You redirected again. By Sunday she was telling neighbors how thorough you were.

options.label: Smile and let it pass
options.flavor: You said 'mm-hm' four times. She left a clipping on the counter — a magazine piece about a school in the next town over. You threw it away after she was gone. You felt small for two days. The clipping, you noted, had been printed on her printer.

decisions.prompt: Hazel has a fever Tuesday morning. The math test you scheduled is the math test you scheduled. The fever is real.

options.label: Push the test. Make soup.
options.flavor: She slept until noon. You sat next to her and read a book that wasn't on the lesson plan. The test moved to Friday. Friday came. She aced it.

options.label: Quiz orally from the couch
options.flavor: You read the word problems out loud. She answered between sips of broth. You got through it. The whole thing felt slightly mean and also like a small victory. You filed it under 'parenting,' which is the folder with most of the things.

decisions.prompt: A dad at the park asks, with the careful politeness of someone winding up to disagree, what you do 'about socialization.' Hazel is right there, building something out of pinecones.

options.label: Answer the real question
options.flavor: You said the co-op meets Tuesdays. You said the library Wednesdays. You said she has more friends than you did at her age. He nodded like he was filing it. You couldn't tell if you were defensive or accurate. Both, probably.

options.label: Just smile and watch the kids
options.flavor: You said 'oh, you know, plenty.' He nodded. You both watched the pinecone tower. It fell over. Hazel started a new one. The dad's question stayed in the air for ten minutes after he left — slightly longer than the pinecone tower.

decisions.prompt: Your spouse's hours are cut by a third. The check on the fridge is the household plan. Should you talk about it tonight, or wait until the weekend?

options.label: Tonight. After the kids are down.
options.flavor: You both pulled out the budget. You both knew it before you started. You wrote down four cuts and one possible side gig. You went to bed less afraid than you'd been an hour before.

options.label: Wait until the weekend
options.flavor: You said 'let's wait.' The week was longer for it. By Friday the conversation had grown teeth it didn't need. You had it anyway. You both wished you'd had it Tuesday.

decisions.prompt: You read a blog post about unschooling and now it's eleven PM and you're rethinking the whole curriculum. You could try one project-based week as a test.

options.label: One week. Lichen and bridges.
scene: A presentation at the kitchen table.
options.flavor: They went deeper than you expected. Hazel asked questions that weren't on any worksheet. Bram learned the word 'symbiosis.' You went back to the textbook on Monday anyway. You weren't ready to give it up.

options.label: Stick with the plan you have
options.flavor: You closed the laptop. You'd had a long enough week. The plan you had was working. The blog would be there next month. The blog is always there next month.

decisions.prompt: Bram is six and the letters are still rearranging themselves on the page for him. The boxed curriculum says he should be reading by now. The boxed curriculum doesn't know him.

options.label: Get him evaluated
options.flavor: The evaluator was warm. The word was 'dyslexia,' said gently. There were resources. You cried in the car for a minute before you started it. Then you started it.

options.label: Give him time and read together
options.flavor: You read every night. You let the worksheet wait. Eight months later he picked up a chapter book on his own and you didn't say anything because you didn't want him to notice you'd noticed.

decisions.prompt: Three of your park-bench parents sent their kids back to school this fall. The group chat is quieter on weekdays. Hazel asks if she can go too.

options.label: Talk it through
scene: A list of pros and cons.
options.flavor: She wanted, mostly, to be where her friends were. You said you understood. You said let's try one more semester. She said okay in the way nine-year-olds say okay when they're keeping a tally.

options.label: Reaffirm the choice. Out loud.
options.flavor: You told her why you'd started. You told her what you saw in her this year. She nodded. She still missed her friends. You still missed your park-bench parents. The kitchen was, however, exceptionally well-organized that fall.

decisions.prompt: A co-op parent emails the group: she'd 'love it if we could move toward all-organic snack week.' Three families have thumbs-up'd. Two have not.

options.label: Reply with a measured counter
options.flavor: You wrote four drafts. The one you sent said 'I love the spirit — could we make it suggested rather than required?' Three people thanked you off-list. The original sender did not.

options.label: Stay out of it
options.flavor: You let the thread go. The new policy passed. Your Friday snack was, that week, a bag of fish-shaped crackers in a Ziploc. The kids did not, you noticed, care.

decisions.prompt: There's a small thing you used to make — pottery, a podcast, line drawings, whatever. It has been a long time. The last time you tried, the kids found you within twenty minutes. You could try again.

options.label: One evening a week. Non-negotiable.
options.flavor: Eight weeks in, you finished a thing. It wasn't very good. You finished a second thing. It was better. You'd forgotten how much fun it was to be bad at something on purpose.

options.label: Maybe when the kids are older
options.flavor: You closed the tab. You went to bed at the kids' bedtime, again. The version of you that made things sent a postcard from one room farther away. You'd answer next year, maybe.

decisions.prompt: Your spouse's company is opening an office two states away. The package is generous. Your co-op, your friends, your house — those are here.

options.label: Pack up. Try the new place.
scene: A new park with no familiar dogs.
options.flavor: The new house was bigger. The first month, Bram kept asking when you were going home. By the third, he stopped asking and started calling the new place 'home' without thinking about it. You took that one.

options.label: Stay. Spouse declines.
options.flavor: Your spouse turned it down. The salary number didn't come up for months. When it surfaced at Thanksgiving, you both pretended it was an old wound. It wasn't quite.

options.label: Negotiate spouse-only commute
options.flavor: Your spouse flew out Sunday nights, back Thursdays. The kitchen table got quieter Mondays. You held the four-day stretches together. By the eighth month you both knew the math didn't quite work.

decisions.prompt: Hazel asks for a phone. The number she names is a year older than the number you were planning. Most of her co-op friends have them.

options.label: Get one with rails
options.flavor: She had it by Tuesday. The first week she showed you everything she texted. The second week she didn't. You expected that and were still a little stung when it happened.

options.label: Hold the line another year
options.flavor: You explained your reasoning. She listened. She did not agree. The group chat she wasn't in continued without her. Her disagreement with this was significant and silent and you could feel it from across the kitchen.

decisions.prompt: The AI tutor product everyone is talking about offers a family plan. The trial works — it really does. The math hour you used to teach could be its math hour.

options.label: Hand math to the tutor
scene: Quiet hours at the kitchen table.
A teacher with a kinder voice than yours.
options.flavor: Bram likes it. He learns. You sit in the other room and listen to him laugh at something the bot said. The bot is, you suspect, slightly funnier than you. You'll have to write better material.

options.label: Keep teaching it yourself
options.flavor: You doubled down. You re-read the chapter the night before. You worked the problems first. You were a worse explainer than the bot in some moments and a better one in others. You started a list of the better ones. The bot is, you note, still funnier.

options.label: Let them use it sometimes
options.flavor: You set it up as one tool among several. Hazel went to it when she was stuck. Bram preferred you. You weren't sure if that was about you or about the bot. You took the win.

decisions.prompt: A friend has an opening that would actually use what you used to do. Mostly remote. Four days a week. You'd have to find afternoon coverage for the kids and rewrite your whole week.

options.label: Take it. Build the scaffolding.
options.flavor: Month one was a scramble. Month three was a routine. Month six you described what you did at work to someone at a party and surprised yourself by having a real answer. The party was, you noted, also surprising.

options.label: Hold for now
options.flavor: You wrote a careful no. The friend understood. The role got filled by someone who'd been waiting for something like it. You'd been waiting for Bram to find his other shoe. Different kinds of waiting.

decisions.prompt: Hazel comes in at 11:47. She said 11:00. She is not drunk and she is not crying and she is also not making eye contact. You can lead with the time or with the look.

options.label: Lead with the look
scene: A long silence broken first by her.
options.flavor: She talked for forty-five minutes about something you hadn't known was bothering her. You almost didn't get to the time at all. When you did, she said 'I know, I'm sorry' in a way that meant she'd been waiting for you to say it. You both went to bed at 1.

options.label: Lead with the time
options.flavor: You started with 11:00. She defended. You escalated. By 12:15 you were both saying things you'd want to walk back by morning. You walked them back by morning. The thing she'd been about to tell you came up a month later, in the car, where neither of you could leave.

decisions.prompt: Hazel mentions, casually, that someone from the co-op is 'kind of' her boyfriend now. She has practiced this sentence. She is watching your face.

options.label: Ask one good question
options.flavor: You asked what she liked about him. She thought about it. She gave you a real answer. You wrote it in the journal later. The journal has gotten thicker the year she got harder to read.

options.label: Set the rules first
options.flavor: You listed three things. She listened politely. You realized halfway through the third that you'd been talking for a while. She said 'okay, can I go now.' You said yes. The version of this conversation where you'd asked a question first didn't happen. You could tell, because she was already in her room.

options.label: Tease her gently
options.flavor: You smiled and said 'kind of, huh.' She rolled her eyes and laughed. The actual conversation came four days later, over dishes, exactly the way she wanted it to.

decisions.prompt: Bram has been quieter for three weeks. Not bad quiet — flat quiet. You can ask, you can wait, or you can call the pediatrician.

options.label: Ask. Then listen.
options.flavor: He told you about something at the co-op you hadn't known about. You didn't fix it on the drive. You listened. You both got fries on the way back. He was lighter at dinner. Not all-the-way lighter. Lighter.

options.label: Call the pediatrician
options.flavor: She listened for forty minutes. She gave you the name of someone. The someone had a six-week wait. You took the appointment. You told Bram. He said 'okay.' He looked, very briefly, relieved.

options.label: Give him another week
options.flavor: You watched. The week didn't help. You called the pediatrician on a Tuesday and wished you'd called the Tuesday before. The waitlist was the same length either week. The watching was the part that cost you.

decisions.prompt: Your spouse hasn't laughed at the dinner table in two weeks. The answer is always 'fine.' The pattern is not fine.

options.label: Take the weekend for them
scene: A friend to watch the kids.
options.flavor: Your spouse cried on Friday night. Laughed at breakfast Sunday. Neither thing was small. You drove home Sunday afternoon listening to a podcast neither of you remembered. It was the best two days of the season.

options.label: Lighter the home load. Quietly.
options.flavor: You took the morning routines for a month. You did the dishes you usually leave. Your spouse noticed in the third week and said so. You said 'I know.' It was, somehow, enough.

options.label: Wait it out
options.flavor: You told yourself it would pass. It mostly did. The next dip came in the spring and was harder. You'd remember this one as the warning you'd let go.

decisions.prompt: Hazel is fourteen. The high-school decision is real now. Dual enrollment at the community college, a private school, or finishing at home — pick a frame for the next four years.

options.label: Dual enrollment
scene: A class schedule that doesn't fit any kitchen table.
options.flavor: She walked into Comp 101 the first morning carrying a notebook you'd watched her label the night before. She came home full of someone else's syllabus. You weren't her main teacher anymore. You were her ride. Different job. Less paperwork.

options.label: Private school
options.flavor: The tuition was the conversation for a month. You signed in May. She started in August. The first week she came home with two friends' names and a story about a teacher you would have liked. The kitchen at 9am was very quiet. You filled it with podcasts. The podcasts were terrible.

options.label: Finish at home
options.flavor: You did the planning differently this year. You leaned on online classes for the things you couldn't teach. You wrote her recommendation letter eight months early because you wanted to get it right. You wrote four drafts. You got it right on the fifth.

decisions.prompt: Hazel's applications need a record. You can register her for the standardized test, or build a portfolio narrative with an evaluator. Most schools take both. A few prefer one.

options.label: The test route
options.flavor: She studied harder than you'd seen her study in a year. She came home from the testing center quieter than she'd left. Her score, when it came, was good. Not the top number. A number that opened doors and closed two.

options.label: The portfolio route
options.flavor: You spent six weekends pulling artifacts together. The evaluator read four years of her work and wrote about her in a way that was true and a little kinder than strict accuracy required. You were glad to have hired her.

options.label: Both. It's hedging.
options.flavor: She did both. She was tired by November. The applications had everything. You filed them on a Sunday and made pancakes Monday. The next move was not yours anymore.

decisions.prompt: Bram, eleven, says, casually, that he doesn't think he wants to go to college. He's been watching videos about welding. He wants you to know.

options.label: Take it seriously, at eleven
options.flavor: You asked him what about welding. He showed you a forty-minute video. You watched the whole thing. You did not say anything about it being early to decide. You did say it sounded interesting. He kept watching the videos. He also kept doing his math.

options.label: Gently keep the door open
options.flavor: You said 'okay, we'll see what eighteen-year-old you wants.' He shrugged. You both knew you'd said the careful adult thing. He kept watching the videos anyway. So did you, sometimes.

decisions.prompt: Your father had a fall. It was minor. It is also, you understand, the first one. He could use someone present for a few weeks.

options.label: Go. Bring the school with you.
scene: A spare room with a kitchen-table desk.
options.flavor: The kids ran your father's house in a way he hadn't seen up close. He watched Hazel teach Bram a science thing one morning and said, later, 'huh.' That huh was worth the trip.

options.label: Send help. Visit on weekends.
options.flavor: You hired a caregiver three afternoons. You drove out Friday nights. By the third weekend he wasn't pretending he didn't need it. You wished you'd gone for the long stay. You also knew why you hadn't.

options.label: Call more. Visit at Thanksgiving.
options.flavor: You called every other day. He was fine, he said. Mostly he was. The fall you didn't witness sat in the back of your mind for the season. You'd remember the season by that fact.

decisions.prompt: Bram's piano recital is Saturday afternoon. So is your one friend's surprise fortieth. Both will notice if you skip.

options.label: The recital
options.flavor: He played his piece. He missed two notes. He didn't notice he'd missed them. You did, and didn't mind. The friend forgave you, and wanted the long version of the story over coffee the next week.

options.label: The party
options.flavor: Your spouse went to the recital and filmed it. You saw the video later that night and felt the specific small ache of not having been the one in the audience. You also had a very good evening. Both were true.

options.label: Half of each
options.flavor: You did the recital, then the party. You missed the cake. You caught the toast. Bram, asked later if you'd been there, said yes. He wasn't wrong, exactly. You'd been there for the parts he was paying attention to. Which were, in fairness, the parts that mattered.

decisions.prompt: Hazel will be away three nights a week next semester for the early-college program. Her bedroom on Tuesdays will look the way it'll look in a few years all the time. You can plan for the Tuesdays or you can sit in them and see what happens.

options.label: Plan something for those Tuesdays
options.flavor: You signed up for a class at the community center. You went the first week and felt slightly embarrassed about how good it felt. You went the second week and didn't. By the eighth week you knew people by name.

options.label: Sit with the quiet for a while
options.flavor: You didn't fill the time. The Tuesday quiet was uncomfortable for two weeks and then it wasn't. You read three books you'd been meaning to read. You realized you'd needed the quiet more than you'd thought.

decisions.prompt: The local homeschool collective puts on a graduation ceremony every June. Caps, gowns, the works. Hazel could walk in it. She is not sure she wants to.

options.label: Throw something at home
scene: A speech you wrote on Wednesday.
options.flavor: Your sister came. Your father came. Hazel cried a little during your speech. She told you afterward she'd been afraid the speech would be embarrassing. She said yours wasn't. The 'yours wasn't' was the line you'd remember.

options.label: Walk in the ceremony
options.flavor: You watched her walk across a small stage with twenty other homeschooled kids. The principal-equivalent said her name. You held it together for the name. You did not for the picture afterward. The picture is, somehow, the one on the fridge now.

options.label: Mark it small, between you
options.flavor: You took her out for breakfast on the actual last day of her last subject. You gave her a small thing wrapped in newspaper. She unwrapped it carefully. She kept the newspaper. You both did, in the way you keep things you don't have a word for yet.

decisions.prompt: A small but determined rebellion formed when snacks were limited. Negotiations were held. Concessions were made. The snacks won.

options.label: Hold the line
options.flavor: You denied the snack. There was a brief revolt. By the end of it, the children had organized themselves into a small protest faction with hand-lettered signs. You were impressed. You did not say so.

options.label: Approve the snack
options.flavor: Snacks were issued. Crumbs were issued shortly thereafter. Bram's shirt acquired a small topographical map of goldfish. You vacuumed twice. Nobody thanked the vacuum.

options.label: Be temporarily unavailable
options.flavor: You closed the office door, audibly. 'Mom is in a meeting,' Hazel announced to no one in particular. Bram believed it for almost ninety seconds. The granola bars stayed in the cabinet, briefly.

decisions.prompt: The kids are complaining that the tablet is too slow.

options.label: Compare it to Windows 98
options.flavor: You told them it was faster than Windows 98 loading a webpage over dial-up. They asked what Windows 98 was. The complaining did not stop. It now had a footnote.

options.label: Hunt for a warehouse deal
options.flavor: You scrolled the warehouse deals for twenty minutes. You found a refurb for eighty dollars. The arrival window was three days. By the time it shipped, the complaining had moved on to something else.

options.label: Confiscate it
options.flavor: You unplugged the tablet, put it on top of the fridge, and went to start dinner. The first request to have it back arrived in twelve minutes. The second came at eight the next morning.
